planting.note: Plant stem cuttings with at least 3 nodes in moist, well-drained, organic, slightly acidic bark or orchid mix in heavy shade; vanilla requires aerial root attachment to a support structure, consistent warmth above 55°F, and high humidity. Zone 10–12 outdoors; excellent in a warm greenhouse or conservatory in zone 9.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in coarse, well-drained, slightly acidic organic bark mix in heavy shade with a support structure for aerial roots to climb — vanilla is the only edible-fruiting orchid and the world's second most expensive spice (after saffron), native to the tropical forest understory of Mexico and Central America where it climbs into the forest canopy in deep shade. The pale yellow-green flowers open one at a time for only a few hours and in most cultivated gardens must be hand-pollinated (the natural pollinators, stingless bees of the genus Melipona, are restricted to Mexico). Successfully pollinated flowers develop into the familiar vanilla bean pods (technically seed capsules) over 9–10 months. After harvest, the pods undergo a complex curing process (blanching, sweating, drying) to develop the characteristic vanilla flavor from the compound vanillin. An extraordinary container or greenhouse specimen for warm-climate gardeners who want to grow their own vanilla extract.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; basswood performs best in rich, moist, slightly alkaline conditions and is more tolerant of chalk soils than most native trees.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; American basswood is one of the most important honey trees in North America — when the intensely fragrant cream-yellow flowers open in June and July, beekeepers consider it the single most important nectar flow of the year, producing linden honey renowned for its delicate, flowery flavor. The fragrance of a flowering basswood in a neighborhood can be detected at a distance of several blocks. The large, heart-shaped leaves, distinctive bract-winged fruiting clusters, and tall, straight trunk make it a notable landscape specimen. Young leaves are edible in spring salads; the inner bark (bast) is one of the best natural fibers for cordage in North America and was extensively used by Native Americans. Tolerates partial shade well, making it suitable for understory planting in very large gardens.

planting.note: Plant container trees in spring or fresh seed in fall in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, clay to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; sugar hackberry is exceptionally adaptable to difficult soil conditions including alkaline soils, clay, and brief flooding.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade — sugar hackberry is the primary Celtis species of the Gulf Coast and Deep South, better adapted to the heat, humidity, and occasional flooding of zone 7–9 than the common hackberry (C. occidentalis). The distinctive warty, corky-gray bark is unmistakable and one of the most interesting bark textures of any native tree. Small orange to purple-black berries in fall are among the most important food sources for migrating songbirds — more than 50 species consume the berries, including yellow-rumped warblers and hermit thrushes. The tree is the exclusive larval host for the hackberry emperor butterfly and American snout butterfly, two distinctive native butterflies wholly dependent on Celtis species. It tolerates urban conditions, compacted soils, drought, and alkaline pH remarkably well. A keystone wildlife tree for the Gulf Coast landscape.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; cucumber magnolia is the most cold-hardy of the deciduous magnolias and tolerates more cold than virtually any other Magnolia species.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — cucumber magnolia is the largest and most cold-hardy native magnolia of eastern North America and the only magnolia native to the Great Lakes region and far north, surviving to zone 3. The greenish-yellow flowers (2–4 inches) in spring are not the showiest, but the distinctive cucumber-shaped bright red-pink fruiting cones in summer that split to reveal brilliant orange-red seeds are highly ornamental. The tree develops an impressive pyramidal to broadly rounded form 50–80 feet tall — one of the largest deciduous trees in the native flora. Crucially, it is a parent in the development of numerous hybrid 'yellow magnolias' such as 'Elizabeth' (with Magnolia denudata), which have transformed the magnolia world. The tree produces a yellow pigment in the wood historically used by Native Americans. An excellent choice for large-scale landscape planting and ecological restoration in zone 3–8.

planting.note: Plant grafted container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; atemoya requires hand pollination for reliable fruit set as natural pollinators (nitidulid beetles native to South America) are absent in most US gardens. Hardy to about 28°F when established.

directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zone 9–11; atemoya is a hybrid between the cherimoya (Annona cherimola) and the sugar apple (Annona squamosa), combining the better qualities of both parents — the large size and richly flavored flesh of cherimoya with the tropical heat tolerance of sugar apple. Atemoya is widely considered one of the finest-flavored fruits in the world, with a rich, creamy white flesh tasting of vanilla custard with notes of pineapple and coconut. It requires hand pollination in most US gardens — transfer pollen from flowers in the male stage to flowers in the female stage in the morning using a small brush. Plant grafted trees for faster fruiting (typically 2–3 years) rather than seedlings. Popular cultivars include 'Gefner' (nearly self-fruitful) and 'African Pride'. An outstanding subtropical fruit for South Florida and Gulf Coast gardens where winter temperatures stay above 28°F.
